title: Probabilistic learning to perform pre-onset individualised prediction of disease severity: application to Veno Occlusive Disease
summary: We advance a new probabilistic supervised learning approach that permits reliable, automated, and early individualised prediction of the severity with which a disease will develop in a prospective patient. The prediction capacity is illustrated via the pre-transplant prediction of the score of severity of Veno Occlusive Disease (or VOD) in the digital twin (DT) of the considered prospective patient, where this score parametrises the severity with which VOD will develop in this patient, after they undergo their Bone Marrow Transplant. The learning of the relationship between the pre-transplant variables, and a severity score variable is undertaken by modelling this relationship as a (random) function that is treated as a sample function of an adequately-chosen stochastic process. The parameters of this underlying process are learnt using a training dataset that is generated using the real-time evolution of retrospective patients in a cohort, with this training dataset subsequently augmented in size by a probabilistic inverse learning of the score of prospective patients. The augmented training set, then permits the learning of the function that capacitates - at the pre-transplant stage - automated prediction of the score of the severity of VOD that characterises the DT of a physical patient in their unique pre-transplant state. This score is subsequently fed back to the real prospective patient as the severity with which VOD will develop in them, after this patient undergoes their transplant. Such a score then permits the treating Haematologist-Oncologists to decide on the treatment regimen, which in this illustration reduces to deciding on treating the patient with Defibrotide. An AI facility is developed to undertake such automated prediction, with the physician inputting the data on the pre-transplant state that characterises the DT of the prospective patient under consideration.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a probabilistic supervised learning approach that uses digital twins and stochastic processes to predict disease severity—specifically Veno Occlusive Disease (VOD) after Bone Marrow Transplant—enabling early, automated, individualized pre-transplant predictions to guide treatment decisions (e.g., Defibrotide therapy).
AI.motivation: To enable reliable, automated, and early individualized prediction of disease severity in prospective patients before treatment, so that clinicians can make informed decisions about treatment regimens (e.g., using Defibrotide for VOD).
AI.method: The relationship between pre-transplant variables and a severity score is modeled as a random function sampled from a stochastic process. A training dataset is built from retrospective patient cohort evolution data, then augmented via probabilistic inverse learning of prospective patients' scores. The augmented set enables learning a function for automated pre-transplant severity prediction in a prospective patient's digital twin.
AI.result: The approach enables pre-transplant prediction of VOD severity scores for prospective patients via their digital twins, which then informs clinical decisions such as whether to treat with Defibrotide. An AI facility is developed to operationalize this automated prediction.
AI.conclusion: The proposed probabilistic learning framework successfully facilitates early, automated, individualized prediction of disease severity pre-transplant, with practical clinical utility demonstrated in the VOD/Bone Marrow Transplant context.

title: DSU-Net: An Attention-Enhanced Dense Skip U-Net for Breast Lesion Segmentation in Mammographic Images
summary:
Breast cancer remains one of the leading causes of cancer-related mortality among women worldwide, making early detection essential for effective treatment. Mammography is the primary screening modality; however, accurate delineation of suspicious lesions remains challenging and subject to inter-observer variability. Automated segmentation methods can assist radiologists by providing consistent and efficient lesion localization.
  This study presents DSU-Net, an attention-enhanced Dense Skip U-Net architecture for automated breast lesion segmentation in mammographic images. The proposed framework integrates dense skip connections and attention mechanisms to improve feature propagation, preserve spatial information, and enhance lesion boundary delineation. Experiments were conducted using the Curated Breast Imaging Subset of the Digital Database for Screening Mammography (CBIS-DDSM). To address severe foreground-background imbalance, a composite loss function combining Dice loss, focal loss, and binary cross-entropy loss was employed during training.
  The proposed model achieved a Dice Similarity Coefficient of 0.9421, an Intersection over Union of 0.8905, an accuracy of 0.9711, and an AUC-ROC of 0.9878 on the validation dataset. Qualitative evaluation demonstrated accurate delineation of lesions with varying sizes and morphologies, while quantitative results confirmed robust discrimination between lesion and background regions.
  These findings demonstrate that DSU-Net provides accurate and reliable breast lesion segmentation in mammographic images and highlights the potential of attention-guided deep learning for computer-aided breast cancer screening and diagnosis.

AI.tldr: DSU-Net, an attention-enhanced Dense Skip U-Net, achieves state-of-the-art breast lesion segmentation in mammograms with 0.9421 Dice and 0.9878 AUC-ROC on CBIS-DDSM dataset.
AI.motivation: Breast cancer is a leading cause of cancer mortality; mammography screening suffers from inter-observer variability, motivating the need for automated, consistent segmentation methods.
AI.method: Proposes DSU-Net combining dense skip connections and attention mechanisms with a composite loss (Dice + Focal + BCE) to handle foreground-background imbalance.
AI.result: Dice=0.9421, IoU=0.8905, Accuracy=0.9711, AUC-ROC=0.9878 on CBIS-DDSM validation set.
AI.conclusion: DSU-Net provides accurate and reliable breast lesion segmentation, highlighting the potential of attention-guided deep learning for computer-aided breast cancer screening.

title: Iterative AI-guided optimisation of selective triple-drug combinations for breast cancer
summary: Personalised cancer therapy aims to tailor treatment to individual tumour profiles, yet tumour heterogeneity and adaptive resistance continue to limit clinical efficacy. Drug combinations offer a strategy to overcome resistance by simultaneously targeting multiple pathways, but their rational design is constrained by the vast combinatorial search space and experimental cost. Here, we present an AI-guided, QSAR-driven iterative optimisation framework that integrates machine learning with automated experimental screening to enable closed-loop discovery of selective multi-drug therapies. Starting from an initial random screen, the system iteratively predicts, tests, and refines three-drug combinations targeting MCF7 breast cancer cells. Incorporation of non-tumorigenic MCF10A cells enables explicit optimisation of tumour-selective efficacy, prioritising regimens that maximise cancer cell killing while sparing healthy cells. Across successive iterations, the framework rapidly enriched for highly selective, high-efficacy combinations, while maintaining chemical and mechanistic diversity and avoiding convergence on a narrow solution space. By continuously learning from experimental feedback, the approach efficiently navigates millions of combinations to identify a small set of validated, tumour-selective regimens. These results establish a scalable proof-of-concept for AI-driven, closed-loop optimisation of higher-order drug combinations, demonstrating how iterative integration of computation and experimentation can enable adaptive and potentially personalised therapeutic design in precision oncology.

AI.tldr: An AI-guided, QSAR-driven iterative optimisation framework integrates machine learning with automated experimental screening to discover selective three-drug combinations for breast cancer therapy, achieving rapid enrichment of highly selective, efficacious regimens while maintaining chemical and mechanistic diversity.
AI.motivation: Personalised cancer therapy is limited by tumour heterogeneity and adaptive resistance; drug combinations can overcome resistance but rational design is hindered by the vast combinatorial search space and high experimental costs.
AI.method: An AI-guided, QSAR-driven iterative closed-loop framework integrating machine learning with automated experimental screening, starting from an initial random screen and iteratively predicting, testing, and refining three-drug combinations against MCF7 breast cancer cells, with MCF10A cells used to optimise tumour-selective efficacy.
AI.result: The framework rapidly enriched for highly selective, high-efficacy combinations across iterations, maintaining chemical and mechanistic diversity while avoiding convergence on a narrow solution space, efficiently navigating millions of combinations to identify a few validated, tumour-selective regimens.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a scalable proof-of-concept for AI-driven, closed-loop optimisation of higher-order drug combinations, demonstrating how iterative integration of computation and experimentation can enable adaptive and potentially personalised therapeutic design in precision oncology.

title: Deterministic access to global viral sequence data enables robust agentic scientific discovery
summary: Public viral genome resources such as the National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI) Virus database are central to outbreak response, evolutionary analysis, vaccine design, and genomic surveillance. Yet many high-value retrieval workflows remain optimized for interactive use rather than deterministic, reproducible programmatic interfaces. This creates a challenge for Large Language Model (LLM)-based scientific agents, where errors in metadata interpretation, filtering logic, or retrieval can propagate into incorrect datasets. To evaluate agentic viral data retrieval, we built VirBench, a manually curated benchmark of 120 queries spanning diverse pathogens, taxonomic levels, and metadata filters. When autonomous AI systems, including Biomni, Claude, GPT, and Edison Analysis, were tasked with these queries without a dedicated retrieval layer, performance varied widely: mean accuracy ranged from 16.9% for Claude Sonnet 4 to 91.3% for GPT-5.5, with newer frontier models showing progress but residual errors remaining consequential. To address this, we built gget virus, a deterministic query framework that formalizes NCBI Virus-style filtering as a reproducible programmatic system. By staging retrieval, applying metadata constraints before sequence download, and retrieving structured GenBank records, gget virus reduces data transfer by more than 98% for high-volume queries while preserving exact-match semantics. Instructing autonomous AI systems to use gget virus increased accuracy to at least 90.0% across all evaluated systems and up to 99.7% for GPT-5.5, improved response stability to 0.92-1.00, reduced error magnitude, and generally decreased runtime and tool calls. Together, this work establishes deterministic data access as critical infrastructure for reliable agentic science and provides a reproducible retrieval layer for robust human- and AI-driven viral genomics workflows.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces VirBench, a benchmark of 120 viral data retrieval queries, and gget virus, a deterministic programmatic framework for NCBI Virus database access. LLM-based agents using gget virus achieved significantly higher accuracy (up to 99.7%) compared to without it (as low as 16.9%), while reducing data transfer by 98%.
AI.motivation: Public viral genome resources like NCBI Virus are central to research but remain optimized for interactive use rather than reproducible programmatic interfaces, creating challenges for LLM-based scientific agents where errors in metadata interpretation and retrieval can propagate into incorrect datasets.
AI.method: The authors built VirBench (120 manually curated queries) and gget virus, a deterministic query framework that formalizes NCBI Virus-style filtering with staged retrieval, metadata constraints before sequence download, and structured GenBank records. They evaluated multiple AI systems (Biomni, Claude, GPT, Edison Analysis) with and without gget virus.
AI.result: Without a retrieval layer, mean accuracy ranged from 16.9% (Claude Sonnet 4) to 91.3% (GPT-5.5). With gget virus, all systems achieved at least 90.0% accuracy (up to 99.7% for GPT-5.5), response stability improved to 0.92-1.00, error magnitude reduced, data transfer decreased by >98%, and runtime/tool calls generally decreased.
AI.conclusion: Deterministic data access is critical infrastructure for reliable agentic science. gget virus provides a reproducible retrieval layer enabling robust human- and AI-driven viral genomics workflows.

title: From Genomes to Algorithms: Neural Network Applications for Palimpsest Detection in Medieval Manuscripts
summary: Biocodicology, the study of biological information preserved in manuscripts, offers new opportunities to examine parchment as both a textual and biological artefact. This study applies non-destructive sampling to isolate and sequence mitochondrial genomes (mtGenomes) from a 14th-century manuscript, Ms. Codex 1629, which contains both single-use and palimpsested folios. We sought to evaluate whether palimpsest preparation, including chemical washing, compromised DNA integrity and whether computational methods could aid in identifying reused parchment. DNA sequencing revealed that both single-use and palimpsested parchments retained sufficient mtGenomes for analysis, with no significant differences in genome coverage or depth. To assess the potential of computational biology in manuscript studies, we implemented machine learning classifiers, including logistic regression and neural networks, to distinguish palimpsests from single-use folios. Models achieved high precision but exhibited reduced recall for the minority palimpsest class, reflecting dataset imbalance. While additional ancient mtGenome samples from palimpsest are required and further testing is needed, this study demonstrates how integrating molecular biology and neural networks highlights new approaches for palimpsest detection and underscores the evolving role of data science in biocodicology.

AI.tldr: This study applies non-destructive DNA sampling and machine learning (logistic regression, neural networks) to analyze a 14th-century manuscript, finding that palimpsest preparation does not significantly degrade DNA and that computational methods can help identify reused parchment, though dataset imbalance limits recall.
AI.motivation: To evaluate whether chemical washing during palimpsest preparation compromises DNA integrity, and to explore whether computational methods can aid in identifying reused parchment in historical manuscripts.
AI.method: Non-destructive sampling of mitochondrial genomes from single-use and palimpsested folios of Ms. Codex 1629, followed by DNA sequencing and machine learning classifiers (logistic regression and neural networks) to distinguish parchment types.
AI.result: Both single-use and palimpsested parchments retained sufficient mtGenomes with no significant differences in coverage/depth. ML models achieved high precision but reduced recall for the minority palimpsest class due to dataset imbalance.
AI.conclusion: Integrating molecular biology and neural networks offers new approaches for palimpsest detection, highlighting the evolving role of data science in biocodicology, though more samples and testing are needed.

title: Structure-guided taxonomic placement of divergent RNA viruses with ViraClass
summary: Metatranscriptomic sequencing has expanded our knowledge of the RNA virosphere far more rapidly than novel viruses can be taxonomically classified. Taxonomic assignment above the family level is particularly difficult because the RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp) is often the only gene retained across RNA viruses yet exhibits little sequence similarity among highly divergent viruses. Here we show that RdRp protein structure retains taxonomic signal at evolutionary depths where RdRp primary sequence similarity has largely collapsed, and that the organization of this signal is consistent with the current ICTV hierarchy. Based on this, we developed ViraClass, a hierarchical framework for RNA virus taxonomic placement that uses RdRp structure for rank-by-rank assignment from phylum to genus, stopping at the deepest rank supported by confidence thresholds, and calibrated structural clustering for viruses that remain outside existing reference space. Across random-split, prospective and taxonomic hold-out benchmarks, ViraClass outperforms sequence-based and genome-content baselines. The largest gains emerge at deep evolutionary distances, in benchmarks that withhold entire families, orders or classes from the reference, where sequence-based methods lose most of their signal. In challenging boundary cases such as the Flaviviridae, ViraClass's structure-based placements capture the taxonomic boundary tensions highlighted by recent phylogenetic studies. When applied to a large collection of previously unclassified RdRp sequences, ViraClass places high-confidence queries into existing phyla and organizes the remainder into compact structural groups. ViraClass therefore provides a scalable approach from large-scale virus discovery to hierarchical taxonomic interpretation, particularly at the deep evolutionary ranges that current sequence-based pipelines cannot reach.

AI.tldr: The paper presents ViraClass, a hierarchical framework that uses RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp) protein structure rather than sequence similarity to classify RNA viruses from phylum to genus, outperforming sequence-based methods especially at deep evolutionary distances where sequence signal collapses.
AI.motivation: Metatranscriptomic sequencing is discovering RNA viruses much faster than they can be taxonomically classified. Taxonomic assignment above the family level is especially difficult because RdRp—the only gene conserved across RNA viruses—shows little sequence similarity among divergent viruses, leaving a gap at deep evolutionary ranges.
AI.method: ViraClass uses RdRp protein structure (which retains taxonomic signal where primary sequence similarity collapses) for rank-by-rank hierarchical classification from phylum to genus, with confidence thresholds and calibrated structural clustering for viruses outside the reference space.
AI.result: Across random-split, prospective, and taxonomic hold-out benchmarks, ViraClass outperforms sequence-based and genome-content baselines. Gains are largest at deep evolutionary distances when entire families, orders, or classes are withheld. It also captures taxonomic boundary tensions in challenging cases like Flaviviridae, and organizes unclassified RdRp sequences into compact structural groups.
AI.conclusion: ViraClass provides a scalable, structure-based approach for hierarchical RNA virus taxonomy, particularly effective at deep evolutionary ranges inaccessible to current sequence-based pipelines.

title: Fixed point compositionality via low-rank gluing rules in inhibition-dominated threshold-linear networks
summary: Brains routinely generate highly flexible and complex behaviors on a relatively stable structure and limited resources. A key mechanism underlying this ability is compositionality, which allows the brain to efficiently decompose complex tasks into simpler, reusable primitives. While network modularity has often been linked to compositionality in biological and artificial networks, a rigorous mathematical characterization of this relationship in nonlinear networks is still lacking. In this work, we formally investigate how structural modularity supports functional compositionality in inhibition-dominated threshold-linear networks (TLNs). We introduce a novel class of modular network assembly called low-rank gluings, where component subnetworks with arbitrary internal connectivity are connected via specific low-rank couplings. We prove that the global fixed points of these networks are constrained to be combinations of the local fixed points of their constituent modules. For a more structured subclass, called rank-1 gluings, we provide a complete characterization that determines which combinations of local fixed points yield global ones. We apply these results to graph-based networks, extending fixed point decomposition rules from combinatorial threshold-linear networks (CTLNs) to the more flexible family of generalized CTLNs (gCTLNs), thereby proving that these structural rules are more robust than initially posited. Finally, we demonstrate that these gluing rules provide a mathematically tractable recipe for engineering compositional dynamics, enabling the construction of networks with a combinatorially large repertoire of predictable attractors that can be understood from simpler component motifs, ranging from compositions of fixed points to compositional limit cycles.

AI.tldr: This paper formally characterizes how structural modularity (via "low-rank gluings") supports functional compositionality in inhibition-dominated threshold-linear networks, proving that global fixed points are constrained combinations of local module fixed points, and demonstrating applications for engineering compositional dynamics with predictable attractors.
AI.motivation: To provide a rigorous mathematical characterization of the relationship between network modularity and functional compositionality in nonlinear networks, which has been lacking despite its observed importance in biological and artificial systems.
AI.method: Introduces low-rank gluings (and rank-1 gluings) as a modular network assembly framework, where subnetworks with arbitrary internal connectivity are coupled via low-rank connections. Provides theoretical proofs for fixed point decomposition rules and extends results to graph-based networks (gCTLNs).
AI.result: Proves that global fixed points of low-rank gluings are constrained combinations of local fixed points; provides complete characterization for rank-1 gluings; extends decomposition rules from CTLNs to gCTLNs; demonstrates engineering of networks with combinatorially large predictable attractors including fixed points and limit cycles.
AI.conclusion: Structural modularity via low-rank gluing provides a mathematically tractable framework for understanding and engineering compositional dynamics in nonlinear networks, with broad implications for neuroscience and artificial intelligence.

title: Nullclines, Subnullclines and the Asymptotic and Transient Attractors in Eco-Evolutionary Dynamics
summary: In the demographic framework, mortality payoff function describes the cost of an interaction and fertility payoff function describes its reward. So while mortality cost depends on opponent's strategy, fertility reward can be affected by the density-dependent juvenile recruitment survival. This motivates an analysis of the eco-evolutionary dynamics of the classical Hawk-Dove game. It is shown that the stable and unstable equilibria (determined by the intersections of frequency and density nullclines) are connected by heteroclinic orbits, which attract nearby trajectories. The resulting bundle of trajectories leads to the discovery of the so-called subnullcines (manifolds placed between frequency and density nullcline) before they converge to the stable rest point. The initial isolated system is then extended by adding environmental seasonality (periodic background mortality), which acts as an external factor. This leads to complex cycling behavior and the subnullclines act as barriers to the propagation of the perturbation (resilience/resistance threshold). Thus, in a way, this paper completes, yet extends, previous works on the eco-evolutionary dynamics of games with demographic payoffs.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the eco-evolutionary dynamics of the Hawk-Dove game with demographic payoffs, revealing heteroclinic orbits, subnullclines as attractors and barriers, and complex cycling under seasonal environmental perturbations.
AI.motivation: To understand how mortality (cost, opponent-dependent) and fertility (reward, density-dependent via juvenile recruitment) jointly shape eco-evolutionary dynamics in the Hawk-Dove game, and to extend the framework to include environmental seasonality.
AI.method: Analysis of frequency-density nullcline intersections, identification of heteroclinic orbits and subnullclines, and extension to periodic background mortality (seasonality).
AI.result: Stable/unstable equilibria connected by heteroclinic orbits; subnullclines discovered as manifolds that attract trajectories and act as resilience/resistance barriers under seasonal forcing, leading to complex cycles.
AI.conclusion: The work completes and extends prior eco-evolutionary game analyses by formally linking demographic payoff structures to dynamical phenomena such as heteroclinic orbits and perturbation thresholds under seasonality.

title: Which Anatomy Matters Under Limited Labels? A Data-Efficient Anatomy-Aware Benchmark for Cardiac Pathology Prediction
comment: ACCEPTED at ICML 2026 Workshop GlobalSouthML (Seoul, South Korea; PMLR 306, 2026)
summary: Numerous medical imaging problems must be solved under limited labels and constrained compute, yet it remains unclear whether performance gains are driven mainly by more expressive models or by better representation of clinically meaningful anatomy. We study this question through a low-data anatomy-aware benchmark for 5-class cardiac pathology prediction on the public ACDC MRI dataset. Using segmentation-derived patient descriptors from the right ventricle, myocardium, and left ventricle, we compare anatomy-specific and multi-structure representations across linear, kernel, and tree-based classifiers. We find that under limited label settings, representation dominates complexity. These results suggest that in resource-constrained healthcare settings, identifying and representing the most informative anatomy may matter more than the increasing complexity of the model alone.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether performance gains in medical imaging under limited labels come from more expressive models or better anatomical representation. Using a low-data benchmark on cardiac MRI data, the authors find that representation quality matters more than model complexity when labels are scarce.
AI.motivation: To understand whether performance improvements in low-label medical imaging are driven by model expressiveness or by clinically meaningful anatomical representation, especially under resource-constrained settings.
AI.method: A low-data anatomy-aware benchmark using the ACDC MRI dataset for 5-class cardiac pathology prediction. Segmentation-derived patient descriptors from right ventricle, myocardium, and left ventricle are compared across linear, kernel, and tree-based classifiers.
AI.result: Under limited label settings, representation dominates complexity — anatomical representation matters more than model complexity.
AI.conclusion: In resource-constrained healthcare settings, identifying and representing the most informative anatomy may be more important than increasing model complexity.

title: The Identity Trap in EEG Foundation Models: A Diagnostic Audit
comment: 28 pages, 6 figures, 8 tables. Code available at https://github.com/Jimmy110101013/fmscope
summary:
Objective. EEG foundation models (FMs) report strong accuracy on clinical resting-state EEG. However, high accuracy under subject-disjoint cross-validation remains ambiguous: it can reflect a genuine clinical biomarker, or subject-identity features that correlate with the label. We name this the Identity Trap and ask whether it can be diagnosed at the representation level before fine-tuning.
  Approach. We propose FMScope, a frozen-representation protocol packaging five diagnostics: variance decomposition, subject-axis erasure, aperiodic 1/f ablation, layer-wise label probing, and within-subject direction consistency. We apply it to three pretrained FMs (LaBraM, CBraMod, REVE) across four datasets in a 2x2 layout: subject relation of label x presence of a consensus cross-subject EEG marker.
  Main results. (i) The Identity Trap is universal: frozen subject-variance is 13-89x a random null in 12/12 pairs, rising in all 12 under fine-tuning (+10 to +63 pp). This dominance is a removable linear axis: erasing it improves label decoding where the label varies within subject (+6 to +12 pp in primary cells; +4 to +27 pp across external cohorts). (ii) Aperiodic 1/f is one subject carrier: removing it drops the subject probe by 9-19 pp on LaBraM and CBraMod. REVE saturates subject identity without measurable aperiodic dependence. (iii) Fine-tuning amplifies label-variance only in cells with a literature-established cross-subject marker.
  Significance. The Identity Trap is a physically-grounded instance of shortcut learning: the preferred cue has a measurable physiological component, and subject-disjoint splitting alone cannot rule it out. FMScope separates gains reflecting a biological marker from those reflecting subject identity.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies the "Identity Trap" where EEG foundation models achieve high accuracy by learning subject-identity features rather than genuine clinical biomarkers, and proposes FMScope—a diagnostic protocol to separate these effects.
AI.motivation: High accuracy in subject-disjoint EEG model evaluation is ambiguous—it could reflect real clinical biomarkers or spurious subject-identity features correlating with labels. Current validation cannot distinguish these.
AI.method: FMScope is a frozen-representation protocol with five diagnostics: variance decomposition, subject-axis erasure, aperiodic 1/f ablation, layer-wise label probing, and within-subject direction consistency. Tested on 3 FMs × 4 datasets in a 2×2 layout of label-subject relation × consensus marker presence.
AI.result: (i) Identity Trap is universal—frozen subject-variance dominates (13-89x null) across all 12 conditions, worsening with fine-tuning. Erasing the subject axis improves label decoding by +6-27 pp where labels vary within-subject. (ii) Aperiodic 1/f is one carrier of subject identity; its removal drops subject probing by 9-19 pp on two FMs. (iii) Fine-tuning amplifies label-variance only when a cross-subject marker is established.
AI.conclusion: The Identity Trap is a physically-grounded shortcut learning problem. FMScope can diagnose and separate representation-level gains from genuine biomarkers vs. subject identity before fine-tuning, providing a tool to audit EEG foundation models.

title: ShallowBench: Benchmarking Generative Drug Design Models on Shallow-Pocket Targets
summary: While generative AI models have demonstrated remarkable success in structure-based drug design, they predominantly rely on deep binding pockets and struggle to sample effective ligands for challenging low-pocketability targets, such as the historically "undruggable" oncology targets KRAS and MYC. To address this gap, we introduce ShallowBench, a strictly curated benchmark of 5,780 shallow-pocket targets extracted from CrossDocked2020. By computing the difference between an Alpha Shape "lid" volume and the underlying protein atom voxel volume, we successfully isolated targets with low concavity while ensuring sufficient surface area for binding. Evaluating various state-of-the-art generative models reveals weaker predicted binding affinity on these low-concavity interfaces. ShallowBench therefore provides a rigorous benchmark for generative biology models and highlights the necessity of new architectural innovations or loss functions capable of navigating these challenging targets.

AI.tldr: ShallowBench is a curated benchmark of 5,780 shallow-pocket targets from CrossDock2020, designed to test generative AI models on challenging "undruggable" targets like KRAS and MYC. Current models struggle with predicted binding affinity on these low-concavity interfaces, highlighting the need for new architectural innovations.
AI.motivation: Generative AI models for drug design fail on shallow-pocket (low-concavity) targets, such as the historically "undruggable" KRAS and MYC oncology targets. No dedicated benchmark existed to systematically evaluate model performance on these challenging interfaces.
AI.method: Extracted 5,780 shallow-pocket targets from CrossDock2020 by computing the difference between an Alpha Shape "lid" volume and underlying protein atom voxel volume, isolating targets with low concavity while ensuring sufficient binding surface area.
AI.result: State-of-the-art generative models show weaker predicted binding affinity on these low-concavity interfaces compared to deep pocket targets.
AI.conclusion: ShallowBench provides a rigorous benchmark exposing current model limitations on shallow pockets, and new architectural innovations or loss functions are needed to navigate these challenging targets.

title: Dependencies and Dataflow in Seed-Filter-Extend Pipelines
summary: Comparing genomes is critical for discovering mutations, tracking evolutionary lineages, and advancing cross-species genomics. Fundamentally, this reduces to an O(n^2) string-matching dynamic programming (DP) problem, a challenge that has driven decades of performance research. However, executing a strict O(n^2) DP algorithm is computationally intractable for genomes spanning millions to billions of base pairs. Consequently, modern aligners rely on global heuristics to identify thousands of candidate similarity regions between species. Unfortunately, these methods are burdened by complex serial dependencies. Once candidate regions are identified, the pipeline executes localized DP alignments, which introduce their own non-trivial heuristics and irregular data dependencies. While parallelizing dense, two-dimensional DP is a well-studied problem, accelerating this end-to-end pipeline is significantly more challenging. Parallelizing across candidate regions and offloading irregular, heuristic-laden local alignments to modern hardware (such as GPUs) remains a major hurdle. In this work, we address the challenge of overcoming these serial bottlenecks by optimizing the global pipeline across regions. We take inspiration from four papers: LASTZ, SegAlign, Darwin-WGA, and SNAP, synthesizing findings across each to inform optimizations, which we either prototype or implement directly in LASTZ.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the challenge of parallelizing genome alignment pipelines by optimizing the global pipeline across candidate regions, drawing inspiration from LASTZ, SegAlign, Darwin-WGA, and SNAP to overcome serial bottlenecks in both global heuristic search and local DP alignment steps.
AI.motivation: Comparing genomes is critical for discovering mutations, tracking evolutionary lineages, and advancing cross-species genomics, but the O(n^2) string-matching DP problem is computationally intractable for large genomes. Modern aligners use global heuristics and localized DP alignments that suffer from complex serial dependencies and irregular data dependencies, making parallelization challenging.
AI.method: The authors optimize the global pipeline across candidate regions by synthesizing findings from four existing papers (LASTZ, SegAlign, Darwin-WGA, and SNAP) and implementing or prototyping these optimizations directly in LASTZ.
AI.result: The work successfully addresses serial bottlenecks in genome alignment pipelines by parallelizing across candidate regions and offloading irregular, heuristic-laden local alignments to modern hardware like GPUs.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates that synthesizing and implementing optimizations from multiple existing approaches can effectively overcome the serial bottlenecks in genome alignment pipelines, making cross-species genome comparison more computationally tractable.

title: The Dark Regulome: Disentangling Predictability from Regulation in Genomic Foundation Models
summary: High-grade gliomas integrate into neural circuits through functional synapses with neurons, raising the question of which noncoding elements shape synaptogenic gene expression in tumor cells. The regulatory program written across the dark genome, what we call the $\textit{dark regulome}$, is the natural substrate to probe, and sequence foundation models offer a zero-shot route through in-silico mutagenesis (ISM); yet likelihood-based scoring is tautologically coupled to local sequence predictability, leaving the regulatory interpretation underdetermined. Across three architecturally distinct foundation models (Caduceus-Ph, HyenaDNA, Enformer) and 30,448 dark genome elements at 92 glioma-relevant loci, we introduce a residualization-and-permutation diagnostic that separates predictability-driven from regulation-driven RIS variance. A sharp 10kb proximal-regulatory horizon survives every control we apply, but the LM-derived element-class hierarchy does not: a six-feature linear baseline matches Caduceus top-decile membership at AUC $= 0.985$. Cross-architecture decomposition cleanly separates a sequence-predictability layer (the two language models co-rank long well-predicted transposable elements) from a regulatory-output layer (Enformer alone retains residual cCRE-discriminative signal), with literally zero overlap between the two top-100 lists. Conservation, brain cis-eQTL, and STRING-PPI cross-checks then anchor what biology survives: top-100 elements across all three models are $3.3\times$ enriched per model for matching brain eQTLs ($p_\mathrm{emp} < 5\times 10^{-3}$), while a tempting transposable-element regulatory layer and a striking NRXN1+NLGN1 protein-pair convergence both fail proper permutation tests once those tests are constructed. We deliver the diagnostic as a general methodological tool for any ISM-based regulatory study.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a residualization-and-permutation diagnostic to disentangle predictability-driven from regulation-driven signals in in-silico mutagenesis (ISM) analysis of the dark genome in high-grade gliomas, revealing that only a sharp 10kb proximal-regulatory horizon and cCRE-discriminative signal survive proper controls, while language model-derived hierarchies collapse against a simple linear baseline.
AI.motivation: High-grade gliomas integrate into neural circuits via functional synapses, raising the question of which noncoding (dark genome) elements shape synaptogenic gene expression in tumor cells. Existing ISM approaches based on likelihood-based scoring conflate local sequence predictability with true regulatory function, leaving interpretation underdetermined.
AI.method: Applied three distinct sequence foundation models (Caduceus-Ph, HyenaDNA, Enformer) to 30,448 dark genome elements at 92 glioma-relevant loci; developed a residualization-and-permutation diagnostic to separate predictability-driven from regulation-driven variance in ISM importance scores; performed cross-architecture decomposition and validated with conservation, brain cis-eQTL, and STRING-PPI cross-checks.
AI.result: A sharp 10kb proximal-regulatory horizon survives all controls, but LM-derived element-class hierarchy collapses (linear baseline matches Caduceus at AUC=0.985). Cross-architecture decomposition separates predictability (co-ranked transposable elements) from regulatory output (Enformer retains cCRE signal) with zero overlap. Top-100 elements show 3.3× enrichment for brain eQTLs, while transposable-element regulatory layer and NRXN1+NLGN1 convergence fail permutation tests.
AI.conclusion: The diagnostic is offered as a general methodological tool for any ISM-based regulatory study; only conservative controls reliably identify true regulatory signals in the dark genome, with many appealing hypotheses failing proper statistical scrutiny.

title: RETROSPECT: RETROsynthesis via Sequential Prediction, and Chemically Transformed-ranking
comment: Accepted at the AI for Science workshop (ICML 2026)
summary: Single-step retrosynthesis needs both accurate first-ranked suggestions and candidate lists that are rich enough for downstream selection. We study this as a proposal-selection decomposition. Our system, RETROSPECT, combines a single Transformer proposal model, which we call the ChemAlign Transformer, with a LambdaMART reranker over structural, reaction-template, upstream-score, and optional DFT-derived descriptors. The generator is trained with hybrid root-aligned and random SMILES augmentation, Pre-LayerNorm, tied embeddings, exponential moving average weights, and a differentiable atom-balance auxiliary loss. On the full USPTO-50K test set of 5,007 reactions, the generator reaches 55.00% top-1 and 86.18% top-10 exact-match accuracy with 99.86% top-1 validity. On the merged candidate-pool benchmark used for reranking, which contains 5,007 test products and about 111 candidates per product, a LambdaMART model trained on the structural feature set reaches 59.4% top-1 with 0.7171 mean reciprocal rank. Feature ablations show that upstream proposal score and template-frequency statistics provide most of the reranking signal, while DFT and reaction-center DFT features provide smaller and less consistent gains. These results support a modular view of retrosynthesis: stronger single-model proposal and learned candidate selection are complementary, and the proposal model can serve as a drop-in component for ensemble systems such as RetroChimera (Maziarz et al., 2024)

AI.tldr: RETROSPECT combines a ChemAlign Transformer proposal model with a LambdaMART reranker for single-step retrosynthesis, achieving 55.00% top-1 and 86.18% top-10 accuracy on USPTO-50K, with the proposal model serving as a drop-in component for ensemble systems.
AI.motivation: Single-step retrosynthesis needs both accurate first-ranked suggestions and rich candidate lists for downstream selection, motivating a proposal-selection decomposition approach.
AI.method: A single Transformer proposal model (ChemAlign Transformer) with hybrid SMILES augmentation, Pre-LayerNorm, tied embeddings, EMA weights, and atom-balance auxiliary loss, combined with a LambdaMART reranker over structural, reaction-template, upstream-score, and optional DFT-derived descriptors.
AI.result: Generator: 55.00% top-1, 86.18% top-10 accuracy, 99.86% validity on USPTO-50K. Reranker: 59.4% top-1, 0.7171 MRR. Feature ablations show proposal score and template-frequency statistics are most important; DFT features give minor gains.
AI.conclusion: Stronger single-model proposal and learned candidate selection are complementary; the proposal model can serve as a drop-in component for ensemble systems like RetroChimera.

title: CaliPPer: quantifying, predicting and improving AI model performance for binding prediction
summary: Binding prediction models accelerate therapeutic antibody and TCR discovery, but their performance on new datasets is unpredictable, often leading to low discovery rates. Density-ratio methods (PAPE, M-CBPE) provide label-free performance estimation for binary classification, but their assumptions and aggregate-only outputs limit binding prediction on neoepitopes, antigen variants and chemical scaffolds. Here we present CaliPPer (Calibration and Prediction of Performance), a post-hoc framework pairing a multi-chain Sample-to-Domain Distance (S2DD) with distance-aware Bayesian recalibration, operating at three resolutions: generalisability score, aggregate performance prediction, and per-sample confidence. Across ten models, eight architectures and two immune-receptor domains, CaliPPer attains distance--performance correlations $|r|=0.80\text{--}0.92$, predicts AUROC/AP/F1 with mean absolute errors $0.008\text{--}0.070$, and improves AUROC by up to $+0.20$ on unseen epitopes/variants. Applied retrospectively to five published TCR, BCR, MHC--peptide and small-molecule studies, CaliPPer raises true discovery rates in all five (e.g.\ $0/5 \to 3/5$ confirmed neoantigens), providing a triage layer between computational prediction and experimental validation.

AI.tldr: CaliPPer is a post-hoc framework that uses distance-aware Bayesian recalibration to predict and calibrate model performance on unseen data, improving discovery rates across immune-receptor and small-molecule binding predictions.
AI.motivation: Existing binding prediction models suffer unpredictable performance on new datasets, leading to low discovery rates. Density-ratio methods (PAPE, M-CBPE) have limited applicability for neoepitopes, antigen variants, and chemical scaffolds due to restrictive assumptions and aggregate-only outputs.
AI.method: CaliPPer pairs a multi-chain Sample-to-Domain Distance (S2DD) with distance-aware Bayesian recalibration, operating at three resolutions: generalisability score, aggregate performance prediction, and per-sample confidence.
AI.result: Across ten models, eight architectures, and two immune-receptor domains, CaliPPer achieves distance-performance correlations |r|=0.80-0.92, predicts AUROC/AP/F1 with mean absolute errors 0.008-0.070, and improves AUROC by up to +0.20 on unseen epitopes/variants. In five retrospective studies, it raised true discovery rates in all cases (e.g., 0/5 → 3/5 confirmed neoantigens).
AI.conclusion: CaliPPer provides a practical triage layer between computational prediction and experimental validation, enabling more reliable discovery in therapeutic antibody, TCR, and small-molecule applications.

title: A Nine-Compartment Nonlinear Epidemic Model with Spline-Based Identification of Time-Varying Transmission and Vaccination Dynamics: Application to the COVID-19 Third Wave in Italy
comment: 23 pages, 4 figures. Submitted to the SIAM Journal on Applied Mathematics
summary: We develop a nine-compartment nonlinear epidemic model incorporating two co-circulating viral strains (ancestral I1 and the Alpha variant B.1.1.7 I2, which is 43-90% more transmissible, c2=1.5), a super-spreader subpopulation, partial vaccine-induced immunity with waning, and explicit hospitalization dynamics with differentiated mortality. Transmission and vaccination rates are treated as time-varying control inputs and identified from Italian COVID-19 data (January-May 2021) via a Piecewise Cubic Hermite Interpolating Polynomial (PCHIP) control-node parameterization, reducing calibration to a fourteen-variable Sequential Quadratic Programming (SQP) problem with monotonicity and box constraints. A parametric bootstrap (n=1000) quantifies parameter uncertainty. The calibrated model achieves R^2=0.966 for active hospitalizations, R^2=0.987 for cumulative fatalities, and R^2=0.999 for cumulative vaccinations. Well-posedness, the basic reproduction number in closed form, and local and global stability of the disease-free equilibrium are established analytically. An L-infinity approximation error bound shows that the PCHIP control-node parameterization converges to the true time-varying parameters at rate O(h^2) as the node spacing vanishes. Local identifiability and a noise stability bound are established via the Fisher information matrix. A sufficient threshold condition proves epidemic decay under time-varying suppression whenever the effective reproduction number remains persistently below one. Sensitivity analyses consistently rank hospital throughput parameters above the transmission rate, providing a mathematical basis for the observation that reactive containment measures cannot prevent a hospitalization peak already driven by the pre-existing latent viral load.

AI.tldr: A nine-compartment epidemic model for two co-circulating viral strains (ancestral and Alpha variant) with super-spreaders, waning vaccine immunity, and hospitalization dynamics is calibrated to Italian COVID-19 data using PCHIP control-node parameterization and SQP optimization, achieving high goodness-of-fit (R² > 0.966) and establishing theoretical properties including stability, identifiability, and convergence guarantees.
AI.motivation: To model the complex dynamics of two co-circulating viral strains (including the more transmissible Alpha variant) with super-spreader subpopulations, waning vaccine immunity, and hospitalization, while developing a mathematically rigorous framework for time-varying parameter identification from real-world data.
AI.method: A nine-compartment nonlinear ODE model was developed with PCHIP control-node parameterization for time-varying transmission and vaccination rates, calibrated via SQP with monotonicity and box constraints, with uncertainty quantified through parametric bootstrap (n=1000).
AI.result: The model achieved R²=0.966 for active hospitalizations, R²=0.987 for cumulative fatalities, and R²=0.999 for cumulative vaccinations. Analytical results include closed-form R₀, stability proofs, O(h²) convergence rate for PCHIP parameterization, and a threshold condition for epidemic decay when Rₑ<1. Sensitivity analyses identified hospital throughput as more critical than transmission rates.
AI.conclusion: Reactive containment measures cannot prevent hospitalization peaks driven by pre-existing latent viral load; hospital throughput parameters are more influential than transmission rates, providing mathematical justification for prioritizing healthcare capacity.
